text.title: Accessibility Audit Pipeline and CI Gate Rules
text.summary: Defines the mandatory audit sequence, CI gate configuration, and VPAT guidance for WCAG 2.2 AA compliance.

rule.statement: Run automated scanning first (axe-core, Lighthouse, pa11y) to clear issues detectable by tooling, then do manual assistive technology testing for interactive components. Never reverse the order.
rule.rationale: Running AT testing before automated scanning wastes expert time on low-hanging fruit that tools catch in minutes. Automated first, human second is the efficient sequence.

rule.statement: Set axe-core as a CI/CD gate failing builds on critical and serious violations only. Moderate and minor violations produce warnings. Do not gate on all violation levels — it creates alert fatigue.
rule.rationale: Blocking on every violation, including cosmetic issues, causes teams to disable the gate. Impact-based gating keeps the signal actionable.

rule.statement: Test interactive components (drag-and-drop, data grids, custom widgets, modal dialogs) with actual screen readers (NVDA + Chrome, VoiceOver + Safari) — no automated tool substitutes for AT testing of dynamic interactions.
rule.rationale: ARIA role validation requires runtime context. Automated tools miss dynamic state changes, focus management failures, and live region announcements.

rule.statement: Document that automated tools cover approximately 60-70% of WCAG 2.2 auditable criteria. The remaining 30-40% — cognitive accessibility, plain language, reading level, complex chart alt text, and custom widget behavior — requires human evaluation.
rule.rationale: Presenting automated results as full compliance creates legal exposure. VPAT authors and QA engineers must know the coverage boundary.

antipattern.description: Using AI overlay widgets (UserWay, AccessiBe) as the sole compliance strategy
antipattern.reason: Overlays do not fix underlying code issues. They are runtime patches that fail when JavaScript is disabled or with certain AT combinations. Legal challenges have succeeded against overlay-only compliance claims.

section.title: VPAT Guidance

rule.statement: A VPAT (Voluntary Product Accessibility Template) drafted by an agent requires human accessibility expert review and sign-off before publishing. Agents draft; humans certify.
rule.rationale: No AI tool generates a legally defensible VPAT without human review. Incorrect conformance claims create procurement and legal risk.

rule.statement: Keep VPAT drafts versioned alongside product releases. An outdated VPAT from a prior version creates legal exposure for the current version.
rule.rationale: Procurement and legal teams rely on current VPATs. A stale document that no longer reflects the product's accessibility state is worse than no document.

section.reference: Run axe-core via Playwright to get violation JSON for audit input
Agent prompt: process axe JSON into developer-facing remediation brief
